templates: {entity} was worshipped here long before the dungeon had its current name. The worship did not stop when the name changed.
The dungeon remembers when {entity} was not a myth. So does whatever lives at the bottom.
{entity}. Even as a story, it has weight. Stories that old accumulate density.
The myths that mention {entity} are all wrong in the same ways. The dungeon has the original version.
Something on the lower floors answers to the name {entity}. Not because it is {entity}. Because the name fits well enough.
{entity} did not die. Things like {entity} do not die. They descend.
You know {entity}'s story. The dungeon knows what happened after the story ended. The ending is not comforting.
The priesthoods that served {entity} left their libraries somewhere below. Their prayers are still active. The dungeon does not understand why this surprises seekers.
{entity} appears in the carvings on this level. Repeatedly. In positions of supplication, not dominance. That distinction matters.
The creature that guards the deepest room was described in myth as {entity}. Myths are approximations. The creature is precise.
{entity}'s power came from being believed in. The dungeon believes in very little, and yet here {entity} is, still functioning.
Every culture named {entity} differently. The dungeon uses the oldest name, which was not the one you learned.
